blocks: A ninth-grade history teacher assigns a primary source document — a Civil War-era letter — to a class that includes four
Chapter 6 — Differentiation and Accessib — One document. Seven different access problems. A planning period of forty minutes.

S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Use Claude to generate reading-level adaptations that preserve the conceptual target
· Draft multilingual scaffolds and glossaries with appropriate verification steps
· Build UDL-aligned choice boards and alternative format options
· Create extension tasks for advanced learners without separating them from the shared learning goal
· Apply a five-question audit to any Claude-generated adaptation before using it

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE.
blocks: · Is the learning target preserved? The intellectual demand may be accessed differently, but it should not be removed.
· Is the support matched to an actual barrier? A simplified vocabulary list for a student who reads fluently but needs con
Does any part of the adaptation do the student's thinking for them? Sentence stems that point to the answer, glossary en these undermine the learning.
· Is the student's dignity protected? A student with a reading accommodation does not need a version that looks different
· Does this accommodation touch anything that requires institutional process? If a student has a formal accommodation plan

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Lowering the floor instead of reducing barriers.
Treating differentiation as deficit labeling.
Accepting Claude's multilingual output without verification.
Using extension tasks as busy work.
Skipping the audit.
